steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question tests Subject-Verb Agreement. The grammatical subject is the gerund phrase “Distinguishing ‘exactly the same’ and ‘nearly the same’ commodities” — gerund phrases are always singular. The em-dash parenthetical (“whether crops, lumber, or precious metals”) is an interrupter and does not change the subject. The blank needs a singular past-tense finite verb.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘were’: A plural past-tense verb that violates subject-verb agreement with the singular gerund-phrase subject. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘was’: A singular past-tense finite verb that correctly agrees with the singular gerund-phrase subject. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘having been’: A perfect participle — a non-finite form that cannot serve as the main verb of the sentence, leaving the sentence without a predicate. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘being’: A present participle — also non-finite and unable to act as a main verb, creating the same predicateless sentence error as Choice C. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
